class_schedule.topic: American Modernism: Hemingway, Fitzgerald, and the Lost Generation
learning_objectives: Identify the formal and thematic characteristics of American modernist fiction
Analyze Hemingway's iceberg theory: the power of omission and understatement
Understand the Lost Generation as a literary and cultural phenomenon shaped by World War I
readings: Hemingway, 'Big Two-Hearted River' (Parts I and II)
Norton Anthology of American Literature, Modernist period introduction

lecture_notes.introduction: American modernism emerged from the devastation of World War I and the dizzying prosperity of the 1920s. Its writers developed new techniques — compression, understatement, symbolic surfaces — to express a generation's disillusionment with inherited values and inherited literary forms.
key_concepts: The iceberg theory: Hemingway's principle that the dignity of movement in a story depends on what is omitted
Hemingway's style: short sentences, concrete nouns, minimal adjectives, action as emotion
The unreliable narrator: Nick Carraway's compromised position as both participant and judge in Gatsby
The American Dream and its corruption: Gatsby as myth and critique
Expatriate modernism: the paradox of American writers who had to leave America to write about it
lecture_notes.demonstration: Close reading of 'Big Two-Hearted River,' identifying what Hemingway leaves unsaid (Nick's war trauma) and how the story's meticulous surface details function as a controlled response to unspoken psychological damage
setup_requirements: Norton Anthology of American Literature, Vol. D
Complete text of The Great Gatsby

review.value: Hemingway's claim that a writer who knows enough can omit things and 'the reader, if the writer is writing truly enough, will have a feeling of those things as strongly as though the writer had stated them'
Gertrude Stein's label for the cohort of writers who came of age during World War I and whose work reflects disillusionment with traditional values, patriotism, and the promise of progress

class_schedule.topic: The Harlem Renaissance: Hughes, Hurston, and Black Modernism
learning_objectives: Analyze how Harlem Renaissance writers drew on African American vernacular traditions — blues, jazz, folk speech — as literary resources
Understand the debate between Du Bois and Hughes over the social responsibilities of Black art
Identify Hurston's use of free indirect discourse to render Black Southern speech with dignity and complexity
readings: Hughes, 'The Negro Speaks of Rivers,' 'The Weary Blues,' and 'The Negro Artist and the Racial Mountain'
Hurston, Their Eyes Were Watching God, Chapters 1-6

lecture_notes.introduction: The Harlem Renaissance was a flowering of Black art, literature, and music in the 1920s and 1930s. Its writers faced a unique challenge: creating art that was both aesthetically innovative and politically engaged, that drew on Black vernacular traditions while demanding recognition from a white literary establishment.
key_concepts: Hughes's blues aesthetic: incorporating the rhythms, repetitions, and emotional directness of blues and jazz into poetry
'The Negro Artist and the Racial Mountain': Hughes's manifesto for artistic freedom from both white expectations and Black respectability politics
Hurston's free indirect discourse: narrating in a voice that blends the narrator's literary English with the characters' vernacular speech
The porch as public sphere: storytelling, signifying, and community in Their Eyes Were Watching God
Double consciousness: Du Bois's concept of seeing oneself through the eyes of a hostile white world
lecture_notes.demonstration: Listening to a blues recording followed by reading Hughes's 'The Weary Blues,' analyzing how the poem incorporates blues structure (AAB stanza, call and response, improvisation) into its literary form
setup_requirements: Complete text of Their Eyes Were Watching God

review.value: Hughes's practice of using blues song structure, rhythm, and themes — loss, resilience, improvisation — as the formal basis for literary poetry, insisting that Black folk art was as valid a foundation for literature as European classical tradition

Hurston's narrative technique blends the narrator's literary voice with the characters' vernacular speech, granting the community's language the authority and nuance of literary prose without condescension

class_schedule.topic: Midcentury American Fiction: Ellison and the Problem of Invisibility
learning_objectives: Analyze Ellison's concept of invisibility as a metaphor for the social erasure of Black identity
Identify the novel's modernist techniques: surrealism, jazz improvisation, and symbolic set pieces
Understand Ellison's argument that Black experience is central, not marginal, to the American experience
readings: Ellison, Invisible Man, Prologue and Chapters 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Invisible Man is the great American novel of identity. Its unnamed narrator is invisible not because of a biological condition but because white America refuses to see him as an individual. The novel uses every modernist technique available — surrealism, symbolism, jazz structure — to dramatize that erasure and the narrator's struggle to define himself against it.
key_concepts: Invisibility as social construction: 'I am invisible, understand, simply because people refuse to see me'
The battle royal: ritualized humiliation as a metaphor for the conditions of Black advancement in white society
Jazz as narrative structure: improvisation, call and response, and the solo within the ensemble
The narrator's journey through false identities: each imposed by an institution that claims to help
Ellison's rejection of protest fiction: art must be complex enough to match the complexity of experience
lecture_notes.demonstration: Close reading of the battle royal scene, analyzing how Ellison layers literal violence, symbolic meaning, and bitter irony to dramatize the impossible position of a young Black man performing for white approval
setup_requirements: Historical context handout on midcentury civil rights

review.value: A scene in which young Black men are forced to fight blindfolded for the entertainment of white civic leaders, then scramble for electrified coins on a rug — a compressed allegory for the degrading conditions attached to Black advancement in a racist society
Ellison argued that reducing fiction to political protest diminished both art and politics; genuine literature must be as complex, contradictory, and ambiguous as lived experience

class_schedule.topic: Confessional Poetry and the Postwar Self
learning_objectives: Identify the characteristics of confessional poetry: personal revelation, psychological intensity, formal control
Analyze how Plath and Lowell transform autobiographical material into literary art
Understand the cultural context: Cold War conformity and the rebellion against it
readings: Lowell, 'Skunk Hour' and 'Memories of West Street and Lepke'

lecture_notes.introduction: In the late 1950s, American poetry turned inward with unprecedented intensity. The confessional poets — Lowell, Plath, Sexton, Berryman — broke the taboo against personal revelation, writing about madness, suicide, family dysfunction, and sexuality with a directness that shocked the literary establishment.
key_concepts: Confessional poetry: the deliberate use of autobiographical material previously considered too private for literature
The persona problem: the 'I' in a confessional poem is always a constructed speaker, not simply the poet
Plath's violent imagery: Holocaust metaphors, resurrection myths, and the body as battlefield
Lowell's formal evolution: from the dense, allusive early work to the looser, more personal Life Studies
Confessional poetry's cultural context: rebellion against the emotional repression of 1950s America
lecture_notes.demonstration: Close reading of Plath's 'Daddy,' analyzing how the poem's nursery-rhyme rhythms create a disturbing tension with its content — the speaker's rage is controlled by the very formal structures it threatens to shatter
setup_requirements: Printed copies of all assigned poems

review.value: Even in apparently autobiographical poetry, the 'I' is a literary construction; confessional poets select, shape, and dramatize personal experience, and the speaker of the poem is not identical to the poet

Plath's controversial use of Holocaust metaphors in 'Daddy' and 'Lady Lazarus' raises ethical questions about appropriating collective trauma for personal expression — a debate that remains unresolved

class_schedule.topic: Postmodern Fiction: Fragmentation, Metafiction, and Play
learning_objectives: Identify the formal strategies of postmodern fiction: metafiction, unreliable narration, fragmentation, pastiche
Analyze how postmodern writers challenge the distinction between fiction and reality
Understand postmodernism as both a continuation of and reaction against modernist experimentation
readings: O'Brien, 'How to Tell a True War Story' from The Things They Carried

lecture_notes.introduction: Postmodern American fiction questions the reliability of storytelling itself. If modernism fragmented narrative to mirror a fragmented world, postmodernism goes further, asking whether narrative can represent reality at all — and what happens when we try.
key_concepts: Metafiction: fiction that draws attention to its own status as fiction
O'Brien's paradox: 'A true war story is never moral' and 'story truth' vs. 'happening truth'
Morrison's rememory: the past as a living, involuntary presence rather than a retrievable memory
Fragmented chronology as a formal enactment of trauma's disruption of linear time
Postmodern historiographic metafiction: rewriting history from marginalized perspectives
lecture_notes.demonstration: Close reading of 'How to Tell a True War Story,' tracking how O'Brien repeatedly tells, revises, and contradicts the same story to demonstrate that truth and fiction are not opposites but interdependent

review.value: Fiction that self-consciously addresses its own conventions, processes, and relationship to reality, often breaking the fourth wall to remind the reader that what they are reading is a constructed artifact

Morrison's term in Beloved for the involuntary return of traumatic past events as if they were present realities — a form of haunting that challenges the linear model of time and the possibility of 'getting over' historical trauma

quizzes.topic: American Modernism and the Harlem Renaissance

questions.question: Explain Hemingway's iceberg theory and demonstrate how it operates in 'Big Two-Hearted River.' What is omitted, and how does the reader sense its presence?
questions.answer: The iceberg theory holds that a story's power comes from what is left unsaid. In 'Big Two-Hearted River,' Hemingway never mentions the war, shell shock, or trauma. Instead, the story's meticulous attention to physical actions — setting up camp, making coffee, fishing — reveals through its very obsessiveness that Nick is using ritual and control to manage overwhelming psychological damage. The reader senses the submerged trauma through the story's unusual intensity about ordinary tasks.

rubric.key: Accurate explanation of the iceberg theory
Identifies what is omitted in the story
Explains how the reader senses the omitted content

questions.question: What is Hughes's argument in 'The Negro Artist and the Racial Mountain'? Who are the two audiences he is addressing, and what does he demand of each?
questions.answer: Hughes argues that Black artists should embrace their racial identity and cultural traditions as artistic material rather than imitating white standards. He addresses two audiences: white readers, whom he tells that Black art does not need white approval; and Black artists, whom he tells to stop aspiring to whiteness and to draw on blues, jazz, and vernacular culture as the foundation for a distinctive and proud Black aesthetic.

rubric.key: Identifies both audiences and what Hughes demands of each
Connects to the broader Harlem Renaissance context

questions.question: What does Ellison mean by 'invisibility' in Invisible Man? How is the concept different from literal invisibility, and what does the narrator's underground retreat at the novel's opening signify?
questions.answer: Ellison's invisibility is social, not physical: the narrator is unseen because white America projects its own assumptions onto him rather than perceiving him as an individual. His underground retreat — a hole in a basement illuminated by 1,369 light bulbs stealing electricity from Monopolated Light & Power — signifies both his withdrawal from a society that refuses to see him and his defiant insistence on making himself visible on his own terms, using the system's resources against it.

rubric.key: Correctly distinguishes social from physical invisibility
Identifies the element of defiance and self-definition

questions.question: What is the 'persona problem' in confessional poetry? Why is it important to distinguish between the speaker of a poem and the poet?
questions.answer: The persona problem recognizes that even in apparently autobiographical poetry, the 'I' is a literary construction. The poet selects, arranges, and dramatizes experience for artistic effect. Confusing the speaker with the poet leads to biographical reductionism — reading the poem as a document of the poet's psychology rather than as a crafted work of art. This distinction matters because it allows us to analyze the poem's formal and rhetorical choices rather than treating it as a confession to be diagnosed.

rubric.key: Explains the distinction between speaker and poet
Identifies the problem with collapsing the distinction
Explains why the distinction enables better literary analysis

major_assignments.title: Critical Analysis Essay
major_assignments.description: Write a 2,500-3,000 word essay that applies a specific critical lens (historical, formal, cultural, or theoretical) to one or two texts from the course. The essay must demonstrate advanced close reading skills and engage with at least three secondary scholarly sources.
objectives: Apply a critical framework to a literary text with sophistication and specificity
Conduct independent research in literary scholarship
Construct a sustained, thesis-driven argument across a longer essay
Engage with scholarly debate rather than merely citing authorities
deliverables: Works Cited page with primary text(s) and at least 3 scholarly sources
Research log documenting search process and source evaluation

criteria: Critical lens is clearly identified and applied consistently
Framework illuminates the text rather than being mechanically imposed
Analysis demonstrates understanding of the framework's assumptions and limitations
Student's own argument emerges from the intersection of framework and text
Textual evidence is abundant, specific, and well-chosen
Analysis of formal features connects to thematic argument
Reading demonstrates sensitivity to the text's complexity and ambiguity
Close reading drives the argument rather than merely illustrating it
Scholarly sources are engaged with substantively, not merely cited
Student positions their argument within existing scholarly conversation
Prose is clear, precise, and appropriate for an advanced academic audience
MLA format is followed accurately throughout
Sophisticated application of a critical framework to the literary text
Detailed close reading driving a sustained argumentative arc
Polished academic prose demonstrating advanced writing skills

critical_analysis_essay.description: Extended critical analysis applying a theoretical framework to a course text

reading_responses.description: Weekly close reading responses to assigned texts

quizzes.description: Quizzes on literary history, terminology, and assigned readings

seminar_participation.description: Quality of contributions to class discussion and seminar presentations

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam with passage analysis, short essay, and comparative question

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory. More than 3 unexcused absences result in a letter grade deduction. Active discussion participation is expected at the 300-level.
course_policies.late_submissions: Essays penalized 5% per day late. Reading responses not accepted late. Extensions require 48 hours advance arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: All written work must be original individual work. Seminar discussion is collaborative; written submissions are independent.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Plagiarism, including AI-generated text submitted as your own, will result in a zero and referral to the academic integrity board.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Norton Anthology of American Literature (Vol. D/E), assigned novels, word processing software, library database access, and LMS access.
